claim_bullets.bullet_text: Society is produced by our wants, and government by our wickedness; the former promotes our happiness positively by uniting our affections, the latter negatively by restraining our vices (raw/1776ThomasPaine.pdf p.1-5).
Government, even in its best state, is but a necessary evil; in its worst state an intolerable one (raw/1776ThomasPaine.pdf p.1-5).
The English Constitution is so exceedingly complex that the nation may suffer for years together without being able to discover in which part the fault lies (raw/1776ThomasPaine.pdf p.1-5).
Monarchy is contrary to natural rights and scripture, and was introduced by the Heathens (raw/1776ThomasPaine.pdf p.1-5).
The true glory of a nation lies in being unlike other nations, particularly in avoiding idolatrous practices (raw/1776ThomasPaine.pdf p.1-5).

source_record_text: Thomas Paine's 'Common Sense' (1776) argues that society arises from human needs, while government stems from human wickedness. It critiques the English Constitution as overly complex and contradictory, and argues against monarchy, tracing its origins to idolatry.
